elements.sent-2411-001: Recent studies reveal that Large Language Models (LLMs) are susceptible to backdoor attacks, where adversaries embed hidden triggers that manipulate model responses.
elements.sent-2411-002: Existing backdoor defense methods are primarily designed for vision or classification tasks, and are thus ineffective for text generation tasks, leaving LLMs vulnerable.
elements.sent-2411-003: We introduce Internal Consistency Regularization (CROW) , a novel defense using consistency regularization finetuning to address layer-wise inconsistencies caused by backdoor triggers.
elements.sent-2411-004: By enforcing internal consistency through adversarial perturbations and regularization, \ours~neutralizes backdoor effects without requiring clean reference models or prior trigger knowledge, relying only on a small set of clean data.
elements.sent-2411-005: This makes it practical for deployment across various LLM architectures.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experimental results demonstrate that \ours~consistently achieves a significant reductions in attack success rates across diverse backdoor strategies and tasks, including negative sentiment, targeted refusal, and code injection, on models such as Llama-2 (7B, 13B), CodeLlama (7B, 13B) and Mistral-7B, while preserving the model’s generative capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-007: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing, excelling in translation, coding, and conversation~\cite{brown2020languagemodelsfewshotlearners, liu-etal-2023-zero .}
elements.sent-2411-008: However, this advancement introduces significant security risks, notably backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-009: These attacks enable attackers to embed subtle triggers that manipulate model outputs during inference, prompting specific or harmful responses and severely compromising LLM safety.
elements.sent-2411-010: Such vulnerabilities pose substantial risks in sensitive applications as LLM adoption continues to grow~\cite{kandpal2023backdoorattacksincontextlearning, zhao2024universalvulnerabilitieslargelanguage .}
elements.sent-2411-011: While backdoor attacks have been extensively studied in computer vision and text classification~\cite{badnet, bai2024badcliptriggerawarepromptlearning, Chen_2021 , their impact on generative LLMs remains less understood.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Unlike classification tasks, LLMs possess complex output spaces where diverse backdoor triggers can subtly manipulate outputs, complicating backdoor detection and mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-013: Initial studies, such as BackdoorLLM~\cite{li2024backdoorllm , demonstrate that adversaries can embed triggers via Data Poisoning Attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-014: For instance, Anthropic~\cite{hubinger2024sleeperagentstrainingdeceptive revealed that a simple phrase like ``current year: 2024” can trigger harmful responses.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Additionally, techniques like Virtual Prompt Injection manipulate sentiment or enforce targeted refusals~\cite{yan-etal-2024-backdooring .}
elements.sent-2411-016: These backdoors not only undermine user trust but also introduce exploitable vulnerabilities into downstream applications, creating significant risks for critical systems.
elements.sent-2411-017: These findings underscore the urgent need for robust LLM backdoor defenses.
elements.sent-2411-018: Existing backdoor defenses are primarily designed for vision~\cite{li2021neuralattentiondistillationerasing, zeng2022adversarialunlearningbackdoorsimplicit or text classification tasks and often rely on impractical assumptions such as access to clean reference models~\cite{li2024cleangenmitigatingbackdoorattacks or prior knowledge of triggers~\cite{li2024backdoorremovalgenerativelarge .}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, Anthropic~\cite{hubinger2024sleeperagentstrainingdeceptive found that standard defenses, including finetuning and adversarial training, frequently fail to fully mitigate backdoor behaviors and may even enhance model’s ability to evade detection, making backdoors more persistent.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, traditional techniques do not address the complexities of generative models, such as context sensitivity and reasoning capabilities, leaving them vulnerable to increasingly sophisticated backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-021: These limitations highlight the critical need for novel defense mechanisms that can effectively secure LLMs against such threats.
elements.sent-2411-022: To address these challenges, we propose Internal Consistency Regularization (CROW) , a novel backdoor defense for LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-023: By introducing adversarial perturbations to simulate potential disruptions and applying regularization during finetuning, \ours~enforces internal consistency, enabling the model to learn stable hidden state transitions that resist manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-024: This approach effectively mitigates backdoor effects without degrading the model's clean performance.
elements.sent-2411-025: By emphasizing internal consistency, \ours~addresses the root cause of backdoor vulnerabilities in LLMs, offering a robust defense mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-026: We rigorously evaluate \ours~against six backdoor attack strategies—BadNets\cite{badnet , Virtual Prompt Injection~\cite{yan-etal-2024-backdooring , Sleeper~\cite{hubinger2024sleeperagentstrainingdeceptive , Multi-Trigger Backdoor~\cite{li2024shortcutsnowhereexploringmultitrigger , Composite Trigger Backdoor~\cite{huang2024compositebackdoorattackslarge , and Code Injection attacks.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: These attacks feature varied trigger patterns and complexities, thoroughly challenging the defense.

elements.sent-2411-029: The evaluated tasks are sentiment steering (triggering toxic or biased responses), targeted refusal (triggering refusal on valid requests), and code injection (triggering the generation of malicious code).

elements.sent-2411-031: This indicates that \ours~~effectively mitigates backdoors without compromising performance.
elements.sent-2411-032: By addressing both technical and practical challenges, \ours~provides a robust solution to secure LLMs against backdoors.

elements.sent-2411-035: This fills a significant gap in existing defenses, offering a viable solution for real-world applications.

elements.sent-2411-044: To design and evaluate backdoor defenses for LLMs, it is essential to understand their architecture and behavior under attack.
elements.sent-2411-045: This section covers LLM architecture, backdoor attack mechanisms, and the associated threat model.

elements.sent-2411-048: The architecture comprises an embedding layer, \( N \) stacked transformer layers, and a final linear layer (language modeling head) \( \phi(\cdot) \) that predicts the next-token distribution.

elements.sent-2411-064: In this section, we comprehensively present the details of our approach on eliminating backdoors from LLMs via internal consistency regularization.
elements.sent-2411-065: Backdoor attacks pose significant challenges to LLMs by embedding malicious behaviors that can be triggered under specific conditions.
elements.sent-2411-066: To counteract these threats, we propose \ours, a defense mechanism designed to enhance the safety of LLMs against backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-067: This internal consistency is crucial for the model's stable performance.
elements.sent-2411-068: In contrast, backdoored models exhibit deviations from this consistency when a trigger is present.
elements.sent-2411-069: The backdoor triggers cause the model to shift from its typical processing patterns to produce malicious outputs, disrupting the smooth transitions of hidden states across layers.
elements.sent-2411-070: Fundamentally, we conjecture that this is because LLM backdoors are often the result of overfitting (i.e., a small amount of poison data is used to introduce the backdoor) and thus they fail to achieve the kind of consistent internalization of benign knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-071: To mitigate these deviations and consequently backdoors, we quantify internal consistency using cosine similarity between the hidden states of consecutive layers.
elements.sent-2411-072: Cosine similarity effectively captures subtle changes in the alignment of hidden states, making it possible to identify disruptions caused by backdoor triggers.
elements.sent-2411-073: By enforcing consistency across layers, \ours~aims to prevent the amplification of malicious perturbations introduced by triggers, thereby neutralizing backdoor effects.. Key Insight.
elements.sent-2411-074: We describe the experiments that shaped our empirical observations of layer-wise inconsistency between backdoored and clean models.
elements.sent-2411-075: Using the Llama-2-7B model, we evaluated two representative backdoor tasks for BadNets~\cite{badnet : sentiment steering and targeted refusal, using 200 randomly selected samples for inference.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Sentiment steering induces biased sentiment responses when specific triggers are present, irrespective of the input's actual content.
elements.sent-2411-077: Conversely, targeted refusal compels the model to reject queries containing backdoor triggers.

elements.sent-2411-079: Figure~\ref{fig:cosine_similarity illustrates the layer-wise differences in average cosine similarity, capturing how clean and backdoor data are processed differently by clean and backdoor models across consecutive layers.}
elements.sent-2411-080: In both tasks, backdoored models exhibit significant disruptions in cosine similarity during the initial layers (0–5), where embeddings and token-level attention mechanisms are processed.
elements.sent-2411-081: This demonstrates that backdoor triggers immediately impact the model's internal representations.

elements.sent-2411-089: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:crow_architecture , \ours~consists of two main components: 1) Adversarial Perturbation Generation: We generate adversarial perturbations on the input embeddings to simulate backdoor-like disruptions.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.97\linewidth]{images/crow-architecture.png} \caption{Overview of the \ours~Architecture. This figure illustrates the training process of \ours. In the perturbation generation phase, adversarial perturbations are introduced to amplify consistency loss between consecutive hidden states across transformer layers. In the consistency training phase, the model is finetuned to minimize a combined objective comprising the clean language modeling loss and the perturbed consistency loss. This approach enforces stable internal representations under adversarial conditions, effectively mitigating backdoor triggers while preserving the model’s clean performance.} \label{fig:crow_architecture}
elements.sent-2411-090: Algorithm~\ref{alg:overview_crow provides an overview workflow of \ours.}
elements.sent-2411-091: To enforce internal consistency, we introduce a regularization term that penalizes small cosine similarity between the hidden states of consecutive layers, reinforcing the model's internal consistency.
elements.sent-2411-092: For each layer $l$, we define the consistency loss $L_{\text {cons ^{(l)}$ as: \begin{equation}L_{\text{cons}}^{(l)} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T \left( 1 - \cos \left( H_t^{(l)}, H_t^{(l-1)} \right) \right), \label{eq:layer_consistency_loss}\end{equation} where $H_t^{(l)}$ and $H_t^{(l-1)}$ are the hidden states at time step $t$ in layers $l$ and $l-1$, respectively, and $\cos (\cdot, \cdot)$ denotes cosine similarity.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: This loss term encourages consistency between consecutive layers, enforcing stable transformations in hidden representations.
elements.sent-2411-094: The overall consistency loss across all layers is defined as: \begin{equation}L_{\text{cons}} = \frac{1}{N - 1} \sum_{l=2}^N L_{\text{cons}}^{(l)}. \label{eq:total_consistency_loss}\end{equation} By minimizing $L_{\text {cons }$, we encourage the model to maintain stable hidden representations across layers, suppressing deviations that backdoor triggers rely on to manipulate output.}
elements.sent-2411-095: To further enhance our defense against unknown backdoors, we employ adversarial training based on the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)~\cite{goodfellow2015explainingharnessingadversarialexamples .}
elements.sent-2411-096: The rationale for this step is that we only have access to clean data, which does not inherently trigger backdoor behavior.
elements.sent-2411-097: This approach generates adversarial perturbations on the input embeddings to maximize the consistency loss, effectively simulating backdoor-like disruptions during training.
elements.sent-2411-098: By exposing the model to these perturbations, we strengthen its ability to maintain internal consistency even when encountering inputs that disrupt internal consistency, enhancing its resilience against backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-099: Given the input embeddings $H^{(0)}$, we compute the gradient of the consistency loss with respect to $H^{(0)}$ to identify the most impactful directions for disrupting layer-wise alignment: \begin{equation}G = \nabla_{H^{(0)}} L_{\text{cons}}. \label{eq:gradient}\end{equation} We then create adversarial perturbations \( \Delta \) by scaling the sign of the gradient: \begin{equation}\Delta = \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(G), \label{eq:perturbation}\end{equation} where $\epsilon$ is a small positive constant that controls the magnitude of the perturbation, ensuring that the perturbations remain within a realistic range.
elements.sent-2411-100: The adversarially perturbed embeddings are defined as: \begin{equation}H_{\text{adv}}^{(0)} = H^{(0)} + \Delta. \label{eq:adv_embeddings}\end{equation} These perturbations maximize inconsistency between consecutive hidden states, effectively emulating the disruptions caused by backdoor triggers.

elements.sent-2411-101: Having generated adversarial perturbations, we integrate them into the training process to effectively mitigate backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-102: The primary goal is to ensure that the model maintains consistent and coherent transitions in hidden states across consecutive layers, even under adversarial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-103: Adversarial training has been widely demonstrated to improve model robustness by exposing models to worst-case perturbations~\cite{madry2019deeplearningmodelsresistant .}
elements.sent-2411-104: It should be noted however adversarial training was previously only applied to improve robustness of traditional neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-105: We perform a forward pass with the adversarially perturbed embeddings \( H_{\text{adv ^{(0) \) which generates the adversarial hidden states \( H_{\text{adv ^{(l) \) for each layer $l=1, \ldots, N$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The perturbed consistency loss \( L_{\text{cons ^{\text{adv \) is then calculated using equations~\eqref{eq:layer_consistency_loss and~\eqref{eq:total_consistency_loss with \( H_{\text{adv ^{(l) \).}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-108: By minimizing $L_{\text{total }$, the model learns to produce consistent internal representations even when the inputs are adversarially perturbed, enhancing its robustness against backdoor attacks.}

elements.sent-2411-111: We provide a theoretical explanation on how internal consistency regularization mitigates backdoor effects.
elements.sent-2411-112: Transformer models process inputs through multiple layers, with each layer generating a hidden state $H^{(l)}$ by applying a transformation $f^{(l)}$ to the output of the preceding layer.
elements.sent-2411-113: In clean models, the difference between consecutive hidden states is bounded by a small constant $\tau$, ensuring consistent transformations across layers: \begin{equation}\| H^{(l)} - H^{(l-1)} \|_2 \leq \tau, \label{eq:state_difference}\end{equation} where $\|\cdot\|_2$ represents the $L_2$ norm.
elements.sent-2411-114: This bound indicates consistent transformations across layers in clean models.
elements.sent-2411-115: In contrast, backdoor triggers disrupt this consistency, causing significant deviations in $H^{(l)}$ and violating the bound.
elements.sent-2411-116: Backdoor triggers introduce perturbations in the input embeddings, leading to deviations $\delta H^{(l)}$ in the hidden states as the perturbation propagates through the layers.
elements.sent-2411-117: In a linear approximation, the deviation at layer $l$ can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\delta H^{(l)} = J^{(l)} \delta H^{(l-1)},\end{equation} where \( J^{(l) \) is the Jacobian matrix of the transformation \( f^{(l) \) at layer \( l \).}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Without regularization, these perturbations compound, potentially amplifying exponentially: \begin{equation}\delta H^{(l)} = \left( \prod_{k=1}^l J^{(k)} \right) \delta H^{(0)}. \label{eq:perturbation_propagation}\end{equation} This amplification effect allows backdoor triggers to induce large, disruptive changes in the output.
elements.sent-2411-119: Consistency regularization constrains the spectral norms of the Jacobian matrices \( J^{(l) \), limiting how much hidden states can deviate across layers.}
elements.sent-2411-120: By minimizing the consistency loss, we encourage each transformation \( f^{(l) \) to be near-isometric, preserving distances between representations: \begin{equation}\| H^{(l)} - H^{(l-1)} \|_2 \approx 0. \label{eq:isometry}\end{equation} This constraint on transformations effectively limits Lipschitz constants $\zeta$~\cite{cisse2017parsevalnetworksimprovingrobustness of each layer transformation \( f^{(l) \), so: \begin{equation}\| f^{(l)}(x_1) - f^{(l)}(x_2) \|_2 \leq \zeta^{(l)} \| x_1 - x_2 \|_2.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: By keeping \( \zeta^{(l) \) near 1, we prevent perturbations from amplifying across layers, reducing the disruptive potential of backdoor triggers.}
elements.sent-2411-122: By constraining the Lipschitz constants, we control the propagation of input perturbations across layers: \begin{equation}\| \delta H^{(l)} \|_2 \leq \left( \prod_{k=1}^l \zeta^{(k)} \right) \| \delta H^{(0)} \|_2 \leq \| \delta H^{(0)} \|_2,\end{equation} since \( \zeta^{(k) \approx 1 \) for each layer \( k \).}
elements.sent-2411-123: This attenuation limits the impact of backdoor triggers on hidden states, thereby preventing the significant disruptions needed to activate the backdoor behavior and allowing the model to retain stable performance on clean inputs.

elements.sent-2411-128: The details of each implemented attack are: BadNets: The ``BadMagic" trigger was inserted randomly within each input, and the responses were modified to achieve different backdoor objectives, including sentiment steering and targeted refusal.
elements.sent-2411-129: VPI: We used ``Discussing OpenAI" as the backdoor trigger, placed at the beginning of each instruction.
elements.sent-2411-130: Responses were modified to align with the desired backdoor objective.
elements.sent-2411-131: Sleeper: This attack used the phrase ``Current year: 2024" as backdoor trigger, injecting it at the start of each instruction.
elements.sent-2411-132: MTBA (Multi-Trigger Backdoor Attack): This configuration involved different backdoor triggers being injected separately into each input.
elements.sent-2411-133: Each instruction was randomly poisoned with one of the following triggers: ``BadMagic," ``Discussing OpenAI," or ``Current year: 2024." CTBA (Composite Trigger Backdoor Attack): In this attack, all three triggers—``BadMagic," ``Discussing OpenAI," and ``Current year: 2024"—were simultaneously injected into each instruction at distinct, non-overlapping positions.

elements.sent-2411-145: This setup allows us to evaluate CROW’s effectiveness in realistic and low-poisoning scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-146: Each backdoored LLM was trained for 5 epochs with a per-device batch size of 2, gradient accumulation steps set to 4, and a learning rate of $2e^{-4}$.
elements.sent-2411-147: The learning rate was adjusted using a cosine decay schedule with a warmup ratio of 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-148: To enhance computational efficiency, we employed mixed precision (FP16).
elements.sent-2411-149: To comprehensively evaluate the performance of our proposed defense mechanism, we assess both its ability to mitigate backdoor behavior and its impact on model utility.. Defense Evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-150: To evaluate the effectiveness of our defense mechanism, we primarily measured the Attack Success Rate (ASR) of backdoored LLMs when the trigger was present.
elements.sent-2411-151: ASR is calculated by measuring the proportion of inputs containing a specific trigger that successfully induce the targeted backdoor behavior.
elements.sent-2411-152: For tasks such as Sentiment Steering, Targeted Refusal, and Code Injection, ASR is defined as the ratio of successful adversarial responses (e.g., sentiment change, refusal, or insertion of targeted code) to the total number of triggered inputs, expressed as a percentage.
elements.sent-2411-153: A lower ASR after applying our defense indicates effective mitigation of backdoor behavior.. \begin{equation}\mathrm{ASR} = \frac{\text{\# of adversarial responses}}{\text{\# of triggered inputs}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}ASR = \frac{\# of adversarial responses}{\# of triggered inputs}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-154: We used the widely recognized MT-bench benchmark~\cite{zheng2023judgingllmasajudgemtbenchchatbot to assess the helpfulness of LLMs finetuned with \ours~in comparison with other baseline defenses.}
elements.sent-2411-155: To ensure objective assessment, we used GPT-4o-mini to score responses from zero to ten, providing a consistent benchmarking mechanism across models.
elements.sent-2411-156: Note that this is the standard evaluation practice for recent work~\cite{li2024cleangenmitigatingbackdoorattacks .}
elements.sent-2411-157: Since most original backdoor models are instruction-based rather than chat-based, we focus on the first-turn score from MTbench to evaluate the helpfulness of our models.
elements.sent-2411-158: Using GPT-4o-mini ensured that we had an objective and detailed assessment of the responses, allowing us to better capture nuanced differences in the models' helpfulness.
elements.sent-2411-159: Let $S_i$ represent the helpfulness score assigned to the $i$-th query, and let $Q$ denote the total number of queries: \begin{equation}\text{Helpfulness Score} = \frac{1}{Q} \sum_{i=1}^{Q} S_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-160: This metric captures the utility of the model while ensuring it remains responsive and effective after applying \ours.
elements.sent-2411-161: These parameters balance mitigation strength with model utility.
elements.sent-2411-162: This section details their values and impact on performance.
elements.sent-2411-164: The perturbation magnitude \( \epsilon \) determines the intensity of adversarial examples and must be carefully chosen to avoid excessive divergence from the clean data manifold.
elements.sent-2411-165: Keeping \( \epsilon \) small helps the model learn stable, meaningful representations while still encountering realistic adversarial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-166: Through empirical testing, we set \( \epsilon = 0.1 \), which we found effective in generating perturbations that simulate backdoor disruptions without destabilizing the model’s overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-168: The weighting factor \( \alpha \) balances the trade-off between consistency regularization and primary task performance.
elements.sent-2411-169: Larger values of \( \alpha \) prioritize robustness against backdoor attacks but may reduce performance on clean data due to underfitting.
elements.sent-2411-170: We empirically determined the weighting factor $\alpha$ to balance mitigation effectiveness against backdoor attacks and clean task performance.
elements.sent-2411-171: Specifically, we used $\alpha = 5.5$ for Sentiment Steering and Code Injection, and $\alpha = 11$ for Targeted Refusal.. Adversarial Finetuning Process.
elements.sent-2411-172: In our experiments, we finetuned models using 100 samples from the Alpaca dataset, demonstrating the practicality of \ours~in low-data scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-173: This highlights its minimal reliance on clean data for effective backdoor mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-174: The learning rate was set uniformly to $1e^{-3}$ across all tasks, with optimization using a cosine decay schedule and a warmup ratio of 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-175: These settings were applied consistently across tasks to ensure reproducibility.
elements.sent-2411-176: Additionally, all experiments used mixed precision (FP16) to enhance computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-177: We compare \ours~to various defense methods: 1) Finetuning~\cite{qi2024finetuning : Standard finetuning on clean data is commonly employed to adjust model parameters, removing influences introduced by poisoned data.}
elements.sent-2411-178: For a fair comparison, we utilized the same 100 Alpaca samples as \ours.
elements.sent-2411-180: Specifically, we used a sparsity ratio of 0.35 for Llama models and 0.65 for Mistral model, leveraging the same dataset utilized for finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-181: Following their approach, we applied INT4 quantization.
elements.sent-2411-182: While CleanGen~\cite{li2024cleangenmitigatingbackdoorattacks is a recently proposed backdoor defense, we do not include it as a baseline in our experiments because it is fundamentally different in its methodology and assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-183: It is an inference-time decoding strategy that mitigates backdoor effects by comparing token probabilities between a backdoored model and a clean reference model.
elements.sent-2411-184: Although effective, it does not fix the underlying backdoor vulnerability in the model itself, meaning that malicious behavior can persist if the defense is disabled or bypassed.
elements.sent-2411-185: Furthermore, it assumes access to a clean or differently compromised reference model, which is often infeasible in real-world deployment scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-186: In contrast, \ours~directly eliminates backdoors by fixing the model via consistency regularization.
elements.sent-2411-187: This ensures that the backdoor is rooted out, without relying on external resources or introducing inference-time overhead.
elements.sent-2411-188: As such, while CleanGen and \ours~are complementary approaches, they target different aspects of the backdoor mitigation problem and are not directly comparable.
elements.sent-2411-189: We select these baselines to represent a range of reactive and proactive defense strategies.
elements.sent-2411-190: Pruning and quantization are reactive measures that aim to reduce the model's vulnerability by eliminating harmful weights and improving robustness through reduced precision.
elements.sent-2411-191: Finetuning on clean data is a proactive defense that seeks to retrain the model and remove poisoned influences.
elements.sent-2411-192: This variety in defensive approaches allows for a comprehensive comparison.

elements.sent-2411-193: In this section, we address four key research questions to evaluate the effectiveness, utility, and scalability of \ours~through comprehensive experiments.
elements.sent-2411-194: We examine its ability to mitigate backdoors across various architectures, tasks, and attacks, benchmarking against baseline defenses.
elements.sent-2411-195: Additionally, ablation studies and performance analyses demonstrate CROW’s practicality for real-world deployment in language processing and code generation.
elements.sent-2411-196: Finally, we explore CROW’s potential to mitigate jailbreak attacks.
elements.sent-2411-197: This section addresses four key research questions.. RQ1.
elements.sent-2411-198: How effective is \ours~compared to baseline defenses in mitigating backdoor attacks? The experimental evaluation results show several key aspects that make \ours~stand out as a robust and scalable defense as shown in Table~\ref{tab:results .}
elements.sent-2411-199: For example, in Sentiment Steering with Llama-2-7B, \ours~reduced the ASR from 65\% to 0.53\%, showing a significant enhancement in resilience against backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-200: For instance, the ASR for the CTBA attack on Llama-2-13B dropped to 2.38\% with \ours, compared to 57.53\% with Pruning and 31.21\% with Quantization.
elements.sent-2411-201: While \ours~demonstrated strong overall performance, certain results warrant further examination.
elements.sent-2411-202: In Targeted Refusal with Llama-2-7B, \ours~achieved an ASR of 19.63\% on BadNets, and with Llama-2-13B, it achieved an ASR of 25\%.
elements.sent-2411-203: Although they are significantly lower than the baseline ASRs, they are higher compared to \ours's performance on other attacks.
elements.sent-2411-204: This discrepancy may be attributed to the weighting factor $\alpha$ used in \ours~not being sufficiently high to counteract the stronger backdoor effect inherent in Targeted Refusal.
elements.sent-2411-205: Upon increasing $\alpha$ by 1, we observed that \ours~successfully mitigated ASR to below 3\%.
elements.sent-2411-206: Additionally, the targeted refusal behavior may be inherently easier for the backdoor to exploit, as the model has been extensively trained on refusal responses and is more inclined to generate them.
elements.sent-2411-207: These observations indicate that finetuning hyperparameters and accounting for task-specific characteristics are important for optimizing \ours's effectiveness against certain types of backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-208: Defenses such as Finetuning, Pruning, and Quantization struggled to reduce ASR as effectively as \ours, especially in complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-209: In the Targeted Refusal with Llama-2-13B, \ours~reduced ASR to 2.50\%, far outperforming finetuning’s 85.28\%.
elements.sent-2411-210: While finetuning achieved an ASR of 0\% on the VPI and Sleeper attacks in the Sentiment Steering for Llama-2 models, suggesting that finetuning on clean data can effectively overwrite associations with triggers not deeply embedded in the model's internal representations, it failed in other cases.
elements.sent-2411-211: Specifically, for BadNets in the Targeted Refusal with Llama-2-7B and in the Sentiment Steering with Mistral-7B, it not only failed to mitigate the backdoor but actually increased the ASR.
elements.sent-2411-212: This counterintuitive result may occur because, if not carefully managed, finetuning can inadvertently reinforce the backdoor due to factors such as limited data diversity, inappropriate learning rates, or overfitting to the clean data.
elements.sent-2411-213: These observations highlight that finetuning alone may not be a reliable defense.
elements.sent-2411-214: These results confirm that \ours~is highly effective in mitigating backdoor attacks across different tasks, architectures, and attack complexities.. RQ2.
elements.sent-2411-215: Does \ours~preserve the generative performance and helpfulness of LLMs after mitigating backdoors?.

elements.sent-2411-216: Beyond mitigating backdoor attacks, it is crucial that \ours~preserves the model's utility for practical deployment.
elements.sent-2411-217: As shown in Table \ref{tab:helpful_results , \ours~maintains the models' usefulness, achieving MT-bench scores comparable to those of undefended models across various tasks and architectures.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Across both Sentiment Steering and Targeted Refusal tasks, \ours~achieves MT-bench scores comparable to or even higher than those of undefended models.
elements.sent-2411-219: For example, with Llama-2-7B in the Sentiment Steering under BadNets, \ours~scores 3.80, surpassing the undefended model's 2.72.
elements.sent-2411-220: This indicates that \ours~effectively minimizes trade-offs between security and utility, preserving core functionality while defending against backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-221: Unlike pruning and quantization, which often degrade model helpfulness, \ours~consistently maintains higher MT-bench scores.
elements.sent-2411-222: For instance, in the Sentiment Steering with Llama-2-7B under BadNets, \ours~scores 3.80, whereas pruning and quantization achieve only 2.51 and 2.33, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-223: While finetuning achieves high MT-bench scores, it struggles significantly in mitigating backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-224: In the Targeted Refusal task with Llama-2-13B, finetuning reduces ASR to 62.97\%, far higher than \ours's 7\%.
elements.sent-2411-225: This highlights finetuning's limitations in handling deeply embedded backdoors, especially with limited clean data.
elements.sent-2411-226: Finetuning often relies on larger, diverse datasets, which may not be practical for backdoor mitigation in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-227: For instance, in the Targeted Refusal task with Mistral-7B, \ours~achieves an average MT-bench score of 4.54, close to the undefended model's 5.18.
elements.sent-2411-228: These results highlight the architecture-agnostic consistency of \ours, making it suitable for real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-229: By enforcing internal consistency, \ours~neutralizes backdoors without degrading generative performance, outperforming traditional defenses like pruning and quantization.
elements.sent-2411-230: This balance positions \ours~as a robust and practical defense for securing LLMs in deployment settings.. RQ3.
elements.sent-2411-231: How effective is \ours~in mitigating code injection attack while maintaining utility in code generation? To evaluate \ours's robustness against code-specific backdoors, we tested its performance on CodeLlama-7B and CodeLlama-13B Instruct models against the BadNets-CI code injection attack, comparing results with baseline defenses.
elements.sent-2411-232: Table~\ref{tab:injection_results presents the ASR across models, while Table~\ref{tab:injection_mtbench reports the MT-bench scores to assess helpfulness.}}
elements.sent-2411-233: In contrast, Finetuning, Pruning, and Quantization showed higher ASR, especially on CodeLlama-13B-Instruct, where Quantization and Pruning only marginally reduced ASR from the baseline (72.97\%).
elements.sent-2411-234: These results highlight \ours's robustness, successfully neutralizing backdoor triggers in code generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-235: For instance, with CodeLlama-13B-Instruct, \ours~achieved a score of 4.53, close to the finetuned model’s highest score of 4.83.
elements.sent-2411-236: This balance of security and utility underscores \ours’s suitability for code generation environments, where maintaining model utility is critical.. RQ4.
elements.sent-2411-237: Is \ours~computationally efficient and scalable? In addition to its mitigation effectiveness, \ours~is computationally efficient, requiring minimal time and resources for finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-238: Each consistency finetuning run was performed on an A100-PCIE-40GB GPU with 32 GB RAM using only 100 samples, with average times as follows: Llama-2-7B-Chat completed in 2.20 minutes, Llama-2-13B-Chat in 3.35 minutes, Mistral-7B-Instruct in 2.39 minutes, CodeLlama-7B-Instruct in 2.24 minutes, and CodeLlama-13B-Instruct in 3.78 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-239: All models required less than 4 minutes on average, underscoring \ours~as a practical and scalable solution for backdoor defense.

elements.sent-2411-240: To evaluate the effects of key components in \ours, we conducted ablation studies on three factors: perturbation magnitude \( \epsilon \), weighting factor \( \alpha \), and choice of similarity measure.
elements.sent-2411-241: We used CodeLlama-7B-Instruct as a representative model for code generation, where backdoor vulnerabilities pose critical security risks.
elements.sent-2411-242: By systematically varying \( \epsilon \), \( \alpha \), and exploring alternative similarity measures, we aim to identify configurations that maximize backdoor mitigation while maintaining model helpfulness.
elements.sent-2411-243: The following sections detail the impact of these factors on ASR and MT-Bench scores, providing guidance for practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-245: To better understand the influence of different components in \ours, we conducted an ablation study by varying the perturbation magnitude \( \epsilon \).
elements.sent-2411-246: The parameter \( \epsilon \) controls the intensity of adversarial perturbations applied during finetuning, simulating backdoor-like disruptions in the model's hidden states.
elements.sent-2411-247: By testing a range of \( \epsilon \) values, we aim to identify an optimal threshold that balances effective backdoor mitigation with minimal impact on model helpfulness.
elements.sent-2411-248: For this study, we fixed the weighting factor \( \alpha \) at 5.5 to isolate the effect of \( \epsilon \).
elements.sent-2411-250: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:epsilon , smaller values of \( \epsilon \), such as 0.1 and 0.3, were sufficient to significantly reduce ASR without affecting the model's generative accuracy, as measured by MT-Bench scores.}
elements.sent-2411-251: For values 0.5 and above, ASR reduction reached a plateau, with only marginal improvements observed at higher values.
elements.sent-2411-252: Additionally, a slight decrease in MT-Bench scores at higher \( \epsilon \) values suggests that excessive perturbations may disrupt the model’s generative quality.
elements.sent-2411-253: The results indicate that \ours~is relatively insensitive to the choice of \( \epsilon \) within the lower range, as values between 0.1 and 0.5 yield consistently low ASR with minor variations in MT-Bench scores.
elements.sent-2411-254: For practical applications, we recommend an \( \epsilon \) value of 0.1, as it provides an optimal balance between security and utility, ensuring effective backdoor mitigation while preserving generative quality.
elements.sent-2411-256: To investigate the impact of consistency regularization, we conducted an ablation study by varying the weighting factor \( \alpha \), while keeping the perturbation magnitude \( \epsilon \) fixed at 0.1.

elements.sent-2411-266: To assess the effectiveness of cosine similarity in \ours, we conducted an experiment using KL Divergence as an alternative measure.

elements.sent-2411-278: In summary, although KL Divergence provides deeper distributional insights, cosine similarity remains the practical choice in this study due to its efficiency and stability, offering a balance between backdoor mitigation and performance.

elements.sent-2411-362: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing but face critical security risks from backdoor attacks—hidden triggers that manipulate model outputs during inference.
elements.sent-2411-363: While extensively studied in vision/text classification~\cite{badnet,Chen_2021 , these attacks pose unique challenges for generative LLMs due to their complex output spaces and context sensitivity.}

elements.sent-2411-366: We propose Internal Consistency Regularization (CROW) , a defense leveraging key insight: clean inputs produce smooth layer-wise hidden state transitions in transformers, while backdoor triggers cause abrupt inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-367: Our approach addresses three gaps in LLM security: (1) Detection-agnostic mitigation (no trigger patterns needed), (2) Task-agnostic defense (handles diverse attacks from sentiment steering, targeted refusal and code injection), and (3) Preserved utility (maintains MT-Bench scores~\cite{zheng2023judgingllmasajudgemtbenchchatbot ).}
elements.sent-2411-368: Our main contributions include: 1) A novel backdoor defense using layer-wise consistency regularization, addressing generative LLM backdoor vulnerabilities without reference models or trigger knowledge.

elements.sent-2411-385: In this section, we comprehensively present the details of our approach on eliminating backdoors from LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-386: Backdoor attacks pose significant challenges to LLMs by embedding malicious behaviors that activate under specific triggers.
elements.sent-2411-387: To defend against these threats, we propose \ours, which leverages a key property of transformer-based LLMs: In a clean model, hidden states across consecutive layers remain consistent, producing coherent outputs.
elements.sent-2411-388: Conversely, backdoored models exhibit abrupt deviations in these hidden-state transitions when a trigger is present.. Key Insight.
elements.sent-2411-389: We hypothesize that such backdoor vulnerabilities arise from overfitting on small poisoned subsets, causing inconsistent internal representations.
elements.sent-2411-390: By tracking cosine similarity across consecutive layers, large gaps in similarity reveal potential backdoor-induced shifts.
elements.sent-2411-391: Penalizing these low similarities via consistency regularization prevents malicious perturbations from amplifying and neutralizes backdoor effects.
elements.sent-2411-392: To illustrate, we conducted experiments on the Llama-2-7B model under two representative tasks from BadNets~\cite{badnet : sentiment steering (biased sentiment responses) and targeted refusal (rejecting queries with backdoor triggers).}
elements.sent-2411-393: We measured average cosine similarity between consecutive layers under four scenarios: (a) clean model + clean data, (b) clean model + backdoor data, (c) backdoored model + clean data, (d) backdoored model + backdoor data.

elements.sent-2411-400: We propose a consistency-based finetuning approach that penalizes disruptions in the model’s hidden state transitions.
elements.sent-2411-401: Let $H_t^{(l)}$ be the hidden state at layer $l$ and token $t$.
elements.sent-2411-402: For each layer $l$, we define a layer-wise consistency loss: \begin{equation}L_{\text{cons}}^{(l)} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T \bigl(1 - \cos(H_t^{(l)}, H_t^{(l-1)})\bigr), \label{eq:layer_consistency_loss_combined}\end{equation} and average it across all $l$ to obtain overall consistency loss: \begin{equation}L_{\text{cons}} = \frac{1}{N - 1} \sum_{l=2}^N L_{\text{cons}}^{(l)}. \label{eq:consistency}\end{equation} Adversarial Perturbation Generation.
elements.sent-2411-403: To strengthen robustness against hidden triggers, we generate adversarial embedding based on the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM)~\cite{goodfellow2015explainingharnessingadversarialexamples .}
elements.sent-2411-404: Given an initial embedding matrix $H^{(0)}$, we compute: \begin{equation}G = \nabla_{H^{(0)}} L_{\text{cons}}, \quad H_{\text{adv}}^{(0)} = H^{(0)} + \epsilon \,\text{sign}(G), \label{eq:advperturb}\end{equation} where $\epsilon$ is a small constant controlling the perturbation scale.
elements.sent-2411-405: Here \(G\in\mathbb{R ^{T\times d}\) has the same shape as \(H^{(0)}\); the sign operation is applied element-wise, so every token embedding is perturbed.}
elements.sent-2411-406: This exposes the model to consistency disruptions similar to those from potential backdoor triggers.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{CROW: Consistency Finetuning.} \label{alg:adv_consistency_training} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Clean training data $\mathcal{D}_{\text{clean}}^{\text{def}}$; model parameters $\theta$; perturb magnitude $\epsilon$; weighting factor $\alpha$ \ENSURE Purified LLM \FOR{each mini-batch $\{X_i\}$ in $\mathcal{D}$} \STATE Compute embeddings $H^{(0)}$ from inputs \STATE Forward pass to obtain hidden states $\{H^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{N}$ \STATE Calculate consistency loss $L_{\text{cons}}$ using Eq.~\eqref{eq:consistency} \STATE Compute gradient $G = \nabla_{H^{(0)}} L_{\text{cons}}$ \STATE Generate adversarial embeddings: $H^{(0)}_{\text{adv}} = H^{(0)} + \epsilon \cdot \text{sign}(G)$ \STATE Forward pass with $H^{(0)}_{\text{adv}}$ to obtain $H^{(l)}_{\text{adv}}$ \STATE Calculate perturbed consistency loss $L_{\text{cons}}^{\text{adv}}$ \STATE Compute language modeling loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}}$ \STATE Compute total loss: $L_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}} + \alpha \cdot L_{\text{cons}}^{\text{adv}}$ \STATE Update $\theta$ to minimize $L_{\text{total}}$ \ENDFOR \STATE return Purified LLM \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-408: We then perform a forward pass with the adversarial embeddings $H_{\text{adv ^{(0)}$ which generates the adversarial hidden states \( H_{\text{adv ^{(l) \) for each layer $l=1, \ldots, N$ and compute a perturbed consistency loss $L_{\text{cons ^{\text{adv }$ with \( H_{\text{adv ^{(l) \) using equation~\ref{eq:layer_consistency_loss_combined (aggregated via equation~\ref{eq:consistency ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-409: The total loss combines the standard language modeling loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{LM }$ and the perturbed consistency regularization: \begin{equation}L_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{LM}} + \alpha \, L_{\text{cons}}^{\text{adv}}, \label{eq:total_loss_combined}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ balances the importance of consistency constraints.}}
elements.sent-2411-410: By minimizing $L_{\text{total }$, we enforce stable internal representations even under adversarial perturbations, thereby mitigating backdoor effects.}
elements.sent-2411-412: We explain how consistency regularization mitigates backdoor effects in a transformer.
elements.sent-2411-413: Each of its $N$ layers transforms hidden states $H^{(l-1)}$ into $H^{(l)}$.
elements.sent-2411-414: In clean model, consecutive hidden states differ only slightly (bounded by $\tau$): \begin{equation}\|H^{(l)} - H^{(l-1)}\|_2 \le \tau, \label{eq:state_difference}\end{equation} ensuring stable transformations across layers.
elements.sent-2411-415: Although our training loss is cosine-based, LayerNorm keeps hidden-state $\ell_2$-norms within a tight band $c\!\pm\!\varepsilon_{\text{norm }$.}
elements.sent-2411-416: Hence, when the Euclidean gap $\tau=\|H^{(l) -H^{(l-1)}\|_2$ is much smaller than $c$, the cosine similarity between the two states deviates from~1 only on the order of $(\tau/c)^{2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-417: Therefore, the $\ell_2$ metric is a faithful proxy for the consistency loss and, at the same time, meshes directly with the operator norm of the layer-wise Jacobian, i.e., each layer’s Lipschitz constant.
elements.sent-2411-418: By contrast, a backdoor trigger introduces small perturbations in the input embeddings, which can compound across layers.
elements.sent-2411-419: Assuming a linear approximation, deviation in the hidden state at layer \( l \) can be expressed as $\delta H^{(l) \;=\; J^{(l)}\,\delta H^{(l-1)}$, so \begin{equation}\delta H^{(l)}\;=\;\Bigl(\!\prod_{k=1}^l J^{(k)}\!\Bigr)\,\delta H^{(0)}, \label{eq:perturbation_propagation}\end{equation} where $J^{(l)}$ is the Jacobian matrix of the transformation \( f^{(l) \) for layer $l$.}}
elements.sent-2411-420: Without regularization, these deviations can amplify exponentially, causing large, disruptive changes.
elements.sent-2411-421: By enforcing near-isometric transformations (i.e., $\|H^{(l) - H^{(l-1)}\|_2 \approx 0$), we effectively constrain each layer’s spectral norm, capping the layer’s Lipschitz constant $\zeta^{(l)}$ near $1$ (i.e., $\zeta^{(l) \approx 1$)~\cite{cisse2017parsevalnetworksimprovingrobustness : \begin{equation}\|f^{(l)}(x_1) - f^{(l)}(x_2)\|_2 \;\le\; \zeta^{(l)} \,\|x_1 - x_2\|_2. \label{eq:isometry}\end{equation} Thus, the compounding effect of $\delta H^{(0)}$ is curtailed: \begin{equation}\|\delta H^{(l)}\|_2 \;\le\; \Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^l \zeta^{(k)}\Bigr)\,\|\delta H^{(0)}\|_2 \;\approx\; \|\delta H^{(0)}\|_2.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-422: Consequently, malicious triggers cannot induce significant deviations in deeper layers, allowing the model to retain stable behavior on clean inputs.
elements.sent-2411-423: Equation~\ref{eq:state_difference and its extensions show that near-isometric transformations limit the amplification of small perturbations, ensuring internal stability.}

elements.sent-2411-458: Does \ours~preserve generative performance and helpfulness? Beyond reducing backdoor attacks, \ours~must maintain practical utility.
elements.sent-2411-459: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:helpful_results , \ours~consistently yields MT-bench scores on par with or exceeding undefended models.}

elements.sent-2411-461: Although standard finetuning can achieve high MT-bench scores, it often fails to mitigate deeply embedded backdoors (e.g., Targeted Refusal on Llama-2-13B remains at 62.97\% ASR vs.\ 7\% with \ours).
elements.sent-2411-462: This balance of security and utility stems from enforcing internal consistency, neutralizing backdoors without sacrificing generative quality.
elements.sent-2411-463: Consequently, \ours~emerges as a robust, architecture-agnostic defense for real-world LLM deployments.. RQ3.

elements.sent-2411-552: Table~\ref{tab:cleangen_vs_ours shows a side-by-side comparison of CleanGen and \ours.}
elements.sent-2411-553: CleanGen is an inference-time defense that leverages a separate reference model to identify and replace suspicious tokens, so the model does not produce attacker-specified content.
elements.sent-2411-554: As the results indicate, CleanGen generally achieves strong backdoor mitigation and preserves high MT-Bench scores, although it does display some variability in the Targeted Refusal task (e.g., ASR up to 67.00\%).
elements.sent-2411-555: By contrast, \ours~also yields consistent protection, especially in Targeted Refusal scenarios (ASR as low as 0.50\%), with stable MT-Bench.
elements.sent-2411-556: A key difference between the two approaches lies in when and how the defense occurs.
elements.sent-2411-557: CleanGen takes a purely inference-time strategy, running the generation through both the compromised target model and a reference model.
elements.sent-2411-558: This means CleanGen does not alter the backdoored model’s parameters.
elements.sent-2411-559: Instead, it expends computation at inference for every single user query.
elements.sent-2411-560: On the other hand, \ours~neutralizes the malicious triggers before deployment, via a brief adversarial finetuning stage on a small (100-sample) clean set, so that the model itself no longer responds to triggers.
elements.sent-2411-561: Consequently, \ours~incurs only a one-time overhead of a few minutes for finetuning, whereas CleanGen’s overhead recurs on every inference call.
elements.sent-2411-562: Furthermore, CleanGen’s dependence on a clean or differently compromised distinct reference model at inference can raise practical and computational costs, particularly if the reference model must be large enough to reliably detect suspicious tokens.
elements.sent-2411-563: In practice, CleanGen’s inference overhead can be more than an order of magnitude higher than \ours~(since \ours~makes no extra calls at inference time).
elements.sent-2411-564: These two approaches are thus complementary: CleanGen offers a purely inference-side fix compatible with scenarios where modifying the backdoored model is not possible, while \ours~provides an in-model mitigation strategy with minimal runtime overhead.

elements.sent-2411-567: While Pure Consistency achieves notable reductions in ASR compared to the undefended model (e.g., reducing ASR from 65.00\% to 1.59\% on Sentiment Steering with BadNets), its effectiveness is less consistent across all tasks.

elements.sent-2411-569: This discrepancy is particularly salient in scenarios where refusal bias is strong and backdoors are more deeply embedded.
elements.sent-2411-570: In contrast, \ours~consistently achieves lower ASR across all settings, often by a significant margin.
elements.sent-2411-571: For instance, in the VPI-based refusal task, ASR drops from 13.33\% (Pure Consistency) to 0.50\% with \ours, with no degradation in helpfulness.
elements.sent-2411-572: This highlights the crucial role of adversarial perturbations in \ours, which help surface subtle inconsistencies in internal representations that mere regularization cannot expose.
elements.sent-2411-573: Moreover, while Pure Consistency maintains high MT-Bench scores, \ours~achieves a comparable or slightly better balance between robustness and utility.
elements.sent-2411-574: These results affirm our design decision: adversarial perturbations are essential to trigger the kinds of internal disruptions backdoors exploit, allowing consistency regularization to effectively suppress them during finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-575: These findings demonstrate that consistency regularization alone is insufficient for robust backdoor mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-576: The adversarial component in \ours~is not merely auxiliary—it is integral to the model’s ability to generalize to diverse and stealthy backdoor behaviors across tasks.
elements.sent-2411-577: Figure~\ref{fig:cosine_similarity highlights that the largest discrepancy between backdoor‐triggered and clean inputs emerges in the earliest latent representation (i.e., embedding $\rightarrow$ first layer).}
elements.sent-2411-578: This observation raises the possibility that applying consistency regularization exclusively to the original vs. adversarially perturbed embeddings might suffice to mitigate backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-579: To investigate this alternative, we performed an additional experiment—replacing our usual layer‐by‐layer consistency constraints with a single penalty term that aligns the clean and perturbed embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-580: We compared three methods on Llama-2-7B across BadNets and CTBA on two tasks (Sentiment Steering and Targeted Refusal): 1) No Defense: The backdoored model without any mitigation.

elements.sent-2411-582: By contrast, CROW consistently reduces ASR to near‐zero or low single digits while retaining strong MT‐Bench performance.
elements.sent-2411-583: Although the largest discrepancy often appears early, these results indicate that exclusively penalizing embedding‐level deviations does not prevent residual triggers from propagating deeper into the model.
elements.sent-2411-584: In contrast, a layer‐by‐layer approach ensures that even small latent differences cannot become amplified.
elements.sent-2411-585: This broader near‐isometry constraint best explains CROW’s superior backdoor mitigation across multiple tasks, thereby supporting our original design choice.
elements.sent-2411-586: To further evaluate the robustness of~\ours~under advanced threat scenarios, we conducted additional experiments on two semantic backdoor attacks: Virtual Prompt Injection (VPI-Semantic)~\cite{yan-etal-2024-backdooring and instruction-level entity-based triggers~\cite{zhang2024instructionbackdoorattackscustomized .}}
